elements.sent-2411-001: This is an overview paper that describes Eliashberg's Legendrian surgery approach to wrapped Floer cohomology and use it to derive the basic relations between various holomorphic curve theories with additional algebraic constructions.
elements.sent-2411-002: We also give a brief discussion of further results that use the surgery perspective, e.g., for holomorphic curve invariants of singular Legendrians and Lagrangians.
elements.sent-2411-003: Starting some time around 2004, Yasha Eliashberg initiated the handle approach to computing holomorphic curve invariants of Weinstein manifolds and their contact boundaries, focusing in particular on relating the most elementary part of closed string symplectic field theory (SFT) \cite{EGH , orbit contact homology, to the simplest part of open string SFT, the Legendrian- or Chekanov-Eliashberg dg-algebra, of the Legendrian attaching spheres for critical handles.}
elements.sent-2411-004: Although perhaps not the original intention, this lead to connections and isomorphisms between all the various flavours of `tree level' holomorphic curve theories for Weinstein manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-005: Corresponding results for `higher loops' constitute very interesting problems where some initial progress has been made but much remains to be understood.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this paper we will explain the steps in the proof of the most important and basic Legendrian surgery isomorphism that expresses the wrapped Floer cohomology of the co-core disks of a Weinstein manifold in terms of the Chekanov-Eliashberg dg-algebra of attaching spheres.
elements.sent-2411-007: We will then discuss extensions and ramifications of this result to related theories: symplectic cohomology with its product structure, Hochschild homology and cohomology of wrapped Floer cohomology, partially wrapped Fukaya categories and Chekanov-Eliashberg dg-algebras with coefficients in chains on the based loop space coefficients, associated dg-algebras for singular Legendrians, and cut-and-paste methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: Consider an exact symplectic $2n$-manifold $X$ with symplectic form $\omega=d\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-009: A vector field $Z$ which is $\omega$-dual to $\lambda$, $\lambda=\iota_{Z}\omega$ is called a Liouville vector field.
elements.sent-2411-010: For such a vector field, if $L$ denotes the Lie derivative, $L_{Z}\omega=d(\iota_{Z}\omega) + \iota_{Z d\omega=\omega$, which means that $\omega$ expands along the flow of $Z$ in the positive direction and contracts in the negative.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The manifold $X$ is a Weinstein manifold if $Z$ is complete and if it admits a Morse function $F\colon X\to\R$ for which $Z$ is gradient like.
elements.sent-2411-012: It follows in particular that outside a compact subset, $X$ is symplectomorphic to $Y\times [0,\infty)$ where $Y=F^{-1 (T)$ for some sufficiently large $T$ and where the symplectic form on $Y\times[0,\infty)$ is $d (e^{t}\alpha)$, $\alpha=\lambda|_{Y}$.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Here $(Y,\alpha)$ is a contact manifold which is the ideal contact boundary of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-014: Compact versions, $\overline X=X\setminus F^{-1 (T,\infty)$ of $X$ are called Weinstein domains, one can move between Weinstein domains and Weinstein manifolds by adding or removing cylindrical ends.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The most basic example of a Weinstein manifold is $\C^{n}\approx\R^{2n}$, the associated Weinstein domain is the $2n$-ball $B^{2n}$, with the standard symplectic form $\sum_{j=1 ^{n dx_{j}\wedge dy_{j = d (xdy)$, Liouville vector field $\sum_{j \frac12\left(x_{j}\partial_{x_{j + y_{j}\partial_{y_{j }\right)$, and ideal contact boundary the standard contact sphere $S^{2n-1}$ with contact structure given by complex tangencies.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Other examples are cotangent bundles $T^{\ast M$ of compact manifolds $M$ with Liouville form $pdq$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Equip $M$ with a Riemannian metric take the Liouville vector field as $\sum_{j \frac12 p_{j}\partial_{p_{j +X_{H}$, where $H=pdq(\nabla f)$ for some small Morse function $f\colon M\to\R$, $X_{H}$ is the Hamiltonian vector field, and the exhausting Morse function is $F(q,p)=\frac12 p^{2 +f(q)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Using the equation $L_{Z}\omega=\omega$, it is straightforward to check that the stable manifolds $W^{\mathrm{s }$ of the critical points are isotropic, i.e., $\omega$ vanishes along $W^{\mathrm{s }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In particular, critical points have indices $\le n$, and ordering the critical levels according to index then gives the following handle decomposition of $X$: \begin{equation}\overline{X} \ = \ \overline{X}^n \ \supset \ \overline{X}^{n-1} \ \supset \ \dots \ \supset \ \overline{X}^{1} \ \supset \ \overline{X}^0,  where  \overline{X}^{m} \ = \ \overline{X}^{m-1} \ \cup_{\Lambda^{m}} \ H^{m}, \quad m=1,\dots,n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-021: Here $\overline{X ^{0}$ is the $2n$-ball and $\overline{X ^{m}$ is obtained from $\overline{X ^{m-1}$ by attaching $m$-handles $H^{m}\approx \bigsqcup \overline{T^{\ast D^{m }\times B^{2(n-m)}$ along a collection of isotropic $(m-1)$-spheres $\Lambda^{m}$ (the descending spheres of the isotropic stable manifolds) in the contact boundary $Y^{m-1}$ of $X^{m-1}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: For $m<n$ there is an $h$-principle for isotropic $m$-spheres in contact $(2n-1)$-manifolds, which means that the symplectic topology of $X^{m}$ is uniquely determined by the homotopy class of the tangent map of $\Lambda_{m}$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Consequently all the interesting symplectic topology of $X=X^{n}$ is carried by the Legendrian isotopy class of the attaching spheres $\Lambda=\Lambda^{n}\subset Y^{n-1 =\partial \overline{X ^{n-1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: This observation can be taken as the starting point for the Legendrian surgery approach to symplectic topology invariants.
elements.sent-2411-025: It says that all symplectic invariants of $X$ can be obtained from $\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-026: We will call the stable manifolds $L$ of the index $n$ critical points the core disks and the unstable manifolds $C$ its co-core disks.
elements.sent-2411-027: The first Legendrian surgery result we will explain can then be stated as saying that the wrapped Floer cohomology $CW^{\ast (C)$ of $C$ is quasi-isomorphic to the Chekaonv-Eliashberg dg-algebra $CE^{\ast (\Lambda)$ of $\Lambda$.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Below we will simplify notation and write $\overline{X =\overline{X _{0}\cup_{\Lambda H$, where $\overline{X _{0}$ is the subcritical part of $X$, i.e., the sublevel set of all handles of index $<n$ and $H$ is the union of critical $n$-handles, one copy of $T^{\ast D^{n}$ for each handle, attached along $\Lambda\subset Y_{0 =\partial\overline{X _{0}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: We will also simplify notation and write e.g., $X=X_{0}\cup_{\Lambda H$, etc, and not distinguish in notation between a Weinstein domain and the Weinstein manifold which is its completion.}
elements.sent-2411-030: We use the notation $Y=\partial_{\infty X$ and $Y_{0 =\partial_{\infty X_{0}$ to denote ideal contact boundaries.}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, we will write $L$ and $C$ for the stable and unstable manifolds of the index $n$ critical points in $H$, and call them the Lagrangian core and co-core disks, respectively.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.5\linewidth]{handles} \caption{A Lagrangian handle, core disk $L$ and attaching sphere $\Lambda$, co-core disk $C$ with Legendrian boundary $\Gamma$.} \label{fig:handles}
elements.sent-2411-033: In this section we give the basic steps in the Legendrian surgery isomorphism connecting the wrapped Floer cohomology of Lagrangian co-core disks to Chekanov-Eliashberg dg-algebras of Legendrian attaching spheres.
elements.sent-2411-034: Consider a contact manifold $V$ with contact form $\alpha$, in our case $V=Y=\partial_{\infty X$ or $V=Y_{0 =\partial_{\infty X_{0}$ and $\alpha$ is the restriction of $\lambda$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The Reeb vector field $R$ of $\alpha$ is the vector field that generates the kernel of $d\alpha$, $\iota_{R d\alpha=0$, normalized by $\alpha(R)=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Flow loops of $R$ are called Reeb orbits.
elements.sent-2411-037: For generic contact form, Reeb orbits are isolated and transverse (linearized return map in general position).
elements.sent-2411-038: For our treatment of holomorphic curve theories we will use parameterized Reeb orbits.
elements.sent-2411-039: This means that we fix a point on each Reeb orbit and consider all its multiples parameterized, starting the parameterization at that point.
elements.sent-2411-040: If $\Lambda\subset V$ is a Legendrian submanifold then Reeb chords of $\Lambda$ are flow lines of $R$ starting and ending on $\Lambda$, again for generic data Reeb chords are isolated and transverse (linearized flow from start point to endpoint in general position).
elements.sent-2411-041: We next introduce the chain complexes underlying our main holomorphic curve theories.
elements.sent-2411-042: We start with the theory after handle attachment.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Generators of the wrapped Floer cohomology complex
elements.sent-2411-043: Consider a Lagrangian $C\subset X$ with ideal Legendrian boundary $\Gamma\subset Y$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Below $C$ will be the co-core disks of a Lagrangian handle attachment, but the definition makes sense more generally.
elements.sent-2411-045: The wrapped Floer cohomology complex of $C$ is most often defined by picking a Hamiltonian displacement of $C$ with prescribed behavior at infinity and taking generators Hamiltonian chords of $C$ and differential counting perturbed holomorphic curves asymptotic to the generators.
elements.sent-2411-046: For Legendrian surgery purposes it is necessary to use the following equivalent definition.
elements.sent-2411-047: Consider a Morse function $f\colon C\to C$ with gradient equal to the Liouville vector field at infinity.
elements.sent-2411-048: Let $C_{-}$ be a time $-\epsilon$ shift along $f$, viewed as a Hamiltonian in $T^{\ast C$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Then the wrapped Floer cohomology complex $CW^{\ast (C)$ of $C$ is generated by Reeb chords starting on $C$ and ending on $C_{-}$ and intersection points in $C\cap C_{-}$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Since the shift is small we see that generators correspond to Reeb chords of $\Gamma$ and critical points of $f$, see \cite[Appendix B.1]{EL .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Before handle attachment we use the Chekanov-Eliashberg dg-algebra $CE^{\ast (\Lambda)$ of a Legendrian $\Lambda\subset Y_{0}$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: It is the algebra of composable words of Reeb chords of $\Lambda$, where a word of chords is composable if the Reeb chord endpoint of a chord lies in the same connected component of $\Lambda$ as the start point of the following chord.
elements.sent-2411-053: Multiplication is concatenation, where the product is zero if the result is non-composable.
elements.sent-2411-054: The basis of Legendrian surgery isomorphisms is the following `homoclinic orbit' observation.
elements.sent-2411-055: Reeb chords of of the co-core boundary sphere $\Gamma=\partial_{\infty C$ are in natural 1-1 correspondence with composable words of Reeb chords of the Legendrian attaching link $\Lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: More precisely, given an action level $\mathfrak{a >0$ there exists a handle size $\delta>0$ such that for all handles of size samller than $\delta$, Reeb chords of $\Gamma$ of action less than $\mathfrak{a}$ are in natural 1-1 correspondence with composable words of Reeb chords of $\Lambda$ of total action less than $\mathfrak{a}$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Outside the handle attachment region the Reeb flows in $Y_{0}$ and $Y$ are naturally identified.
elements.sent-2411-058: We take the co-core as the fiber of $T^{\ast D$ at the center of $D$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Inside the handle the Reeb flow is the lift of the geodesic flow.
elements.sent-2411-060: In particular, the Reeb flow takes fiber hemispheres of the attaching neighborhood to the boundary of the attaching region by a degree one map.
elements.sent-2411-061: A straightforward fixed point argument now establishes the 1-1 correspondence, see \cite[Section 5]{Ecurves for details.}
elements.sent-2411-062: We first consider the differential $d$ in the wrapped Floer cohomology complex $CW^{\ast (C)$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: This differential counts anchored holomorhic strips with a positive and negative puncture at a Reeb chord connecting $\Gamma_{-}$ to $\Gamma$ or an intersection point between $C_{-}$ and $C$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Here anchoring means that we consider disks with additional negative punctures at marked orbits and pure chords where we fill with rigid punctured spheres and disks.
elements.sent-2411-065: The basic property of the curve counting map is the following.
elements.sent-2411-066: The map $d\colon CW^{\ast (C)\to CW^{\ast (C)$ is a differential $d^2=0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The square of the differential counts the ends of a 1-dimensional moduli space, see \cite[Appendix B.1]{EL .}
elements.sent-2411-068: In fact, the differential is the $\mu_{1}$-operation in an $A_{\infty}$-algebra structure on $CW^{\ast (C)$, where the operation $\mu_{k}$ is defined by choosing $k$ parallel copies of $C$, where $C_{-}$ is the first parallel copy which is the time $-1$ shift along the function $\epsilon f_{1}$ for $f_{1 =f$ and the $k^{\rm th}$ copy is the time $-1$ shift along the function \begin{equation}\left(\sum_{r=1}^{k} \epsilon^{k}\right) f_{1} + \left(\sum_{r=2}^{k} \epsilon^{3+r}\right)f_{2} + \dots + \epsilon^{k+1}f_{k}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Then the $\mu_{k}$-operation counts disks with positive punctures connecting copies in increasing order and one negative output puncture, see Figure \ref{fig:CWdiff .}
elements.sent-2411-070: We then have the following result.
elements.sent-2411-071: The operations $\mu_{k}\colon CW^{\ast (C)^{\otimes k}\to CW^{\ast (C)$ satisfy the $A_{\infty}$-relations.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Again the compositions count the ends of a 1-dimensional moduli space.
elements.sent-2411-073: The proof uses that for sufficiently close parallel copies, moduli spaces with boundary components on Lagrangians corresponding to any increasing numbering in the system of parallel copies are be canonically identified.
elements.sent-2411-074: This follows from the form of the shifting functions and the fact that transversely cut out solutions persist under sufficiently small perturbation, see \cite[Appendix B.1]{EL .}

elements.sent-2411-076: The differential counts holomorphic disks in the symplectization $Y_{0}\times \R$ anchored at orbits with one positive and several negative punctures, see Figure \ref{fig:CEdiff .}

elements.sent-2411-078: The map $\partial\colon CE^{\ast (\Lambda)\to CE^{\ast (\Lambda)$ is a differential, $\partial^{2 =0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The square of the differential counts ends of a 1-dimensional moduli space, see e.g., \cite{EL .}
elements.sent-2411-080: The cobordism $W=X\setminus X_{0}$, with the Lagrangians $L$ and $C$ with connected components that intersect at single points in the middle of each handle, gives a natural chain map \begin{equation}\label{eq:CW to CE} \Phi^{CW}\colon CW^{\ast}(C)\to CE^{\ast}(\Lambda),\quad \Phi^{CW}=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\Phi^{CW}_{j},\end{equation} More precisley, $\Phi_{k ^{CW}$ counts holomorphic curves with $k$ positive punctures at Reeb chords of $C$, two punctures where the map is asymptotic to intersections in $C\cap L$ and several negative punctures at Reeb chords of $\Lambda$, where moduli spaces of many positive punctures of $C$ are defined using systems of parallel copies as in the definition of the $A_{\infty}$-operations in order to avoid `boundary breaking', see Figure \ref{fig:CWtoCE .}}

elements.sent-2411-095: The Legendrian surgery proof is powerful and can be used to derive many closely related results.
elements.sent-2411-096: In this section we will discuss some of them.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Symplectic homology and linearized contact homology
elements.sent-2411-097: Changing the surgery isomorphism we can instead map into symplectic cohomology.
elements.sent-2411-098: The standard construction of symplectic cohomology of $X$ starts from a time dependent Hamiltonian with standard linear behavior at infinity of slope not in the action spectrum of the contact form.
elements.sent-2411-099: The complex is generated by Hamiltonian orbits and the differential counts solutions to the Cauchy-Riemann equations perturbed by the Hamiltonian vector field on cylinders that interpolate between orbits.
elements.sent-2411-100: Here we will use a version of symplectic cohomology that arises by first making the Hamiltonian time independent, where time independent orbits is viewed as Bott degenerate time dependent orbits, and then turning the Hamiltonian off, where perturbed curves become standard holomorphic curves with asymptotic markers remebering the Morse theory on the orbits.
elements.sent-2411-101: Our complex for symplectic homology is thus Reeb orbits together with a Morse complex of the underlying geometric orbit and critical points of a Morse function on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-102: The differential counts anchored holomorhic cylinders and spheres with Morse data at orbits, we call these decorated orbits.
elements.sent-2411-103: We denote the corresponding complex $SC^{\ast (X)$, see \cite[Section 3.3]{BEE .}}
elements.sent-2411-104: We next want to model this complex before surgery.

elements.sent-2411-148: We denote this complex \begin{equation}\Ho CW^{\ast}(C)\oplus SC^{\ast}(X)\end{equation} and in analogy with Theorem \ref{t:SC to HCF we have a natural chain map \begin{equation}\Psi\colon \Ho CW^{\ast}(C)\oplus SC^{\ast}(X) \ \to \ SC^{\ast}(X_{0})\end{equation} which is a chain isomorhism.}
elements.sent-2411-149: The connecting homomorphism in the long exact sequence associated to the short exact sequence \begin{equation}0 \ \to \ SC^{\ast}(X) \ \to \ \Ho CW^{\ast}(C)\oplus SC^{\ast}(X) \ \to \ \Ho CW^{\ast}(C) \ \to \ 0\end{equation} is called the open-closed map, see \cite{Ab,G .}

elements.subsection-2411-009: The closed-open map and isomorphisms of Hochschild homology and cohomology
elements.sent-2411-153: The open-closed and closed-open maps were originally studied in the setting of wrapped Floer cohomology from the Hamiltonian view point, see \cite{G,Ab .}
elements.sent-2411-154: Here we continue instead with the Legendrian surgery perspective and use wrapped Floer cohomology without Hamiltonian, see \cite[Appendix B.1]{EL .}
elements.sent-2411-155: The Hochschild chain complex $\Ho CW^{\ast (C)$ above can be thought of as generated by words of negative punctures at Reeb chords along the boundary of a formal disk (one of which is distinguished, we take it to be mixed).}
elements.sent-2411-156: The Hochschild differential then attaches holomorphic disks with several positive and one negative puncture to such words in all possible ways (the negative and one of the positive punctures mixed).
elements.sent-2411-157: Similarly, we consider the Hochschild cochain complex $\Ho' CW^{\ast (C)$ generated by chords along the boundary of a formal disk, all positive except one which is negative (as in the differential the negative and one positive puncture mixed).}
elements.sent-2411-158: The differential on $\Ho' CW^{\ast (C)$ is obtained by attaching the disks corresponding to the differential on $\Ho CW^{\ast (C)$ with one negative and several positive punctures in all possible ways, from above and below.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: The complex $\Ho' CW^{\ast (C)$ has a natural product which acts by gluing negative and positive punctures of one to the other.}
elements.sent-2411-160: This product has a natural unit $e$ which is the sum of words of two punctured disks with the same chord at the positive and negative puncture.
elements.sent-2411-161: Furthermore, there is a curve counting map, the closed-open map \begin{equation}CO\colon SC^{\ast}(X)\to \Ho' CW^{\ast}(C)\end{equation} which counts disks with a positive puncture with marker at the center mapping to an orbit with Morse decoration, one mixed negative boundary puncture at a fixed position, any number of positive boundary punctures with the mixed chord at the point opposite the fixed position, see Figure \ref{fig:CO .}
elements.sent-2411-162: The map $\mathcal{CO}$ is a chain map that respects the product (on homology).
elements.sent-2411-163: The symplectic cohomology product followed by the isomorphism gives a disk with two positive punctures.
elements.sent-2411-164: Looking at possible splittings we find the product on the Hochschild cochains up to exact terms.
elements.sent-2411-166: This is naturally a $\Ho'CW^{\ast (C)$-module: if $a\in \Ho CW^{\ast (C)$ is a cyclic word and $r\in\Ho' CW^{\ast (C)$ then $r\cdot a$ is the sum of cyclic words obtained by attaching the positive punctures of $r$ to consecutive (negative) punctures of $a$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-167: Precomposing the product by the map $\mathcal{CO}$ we find that $\Ho CW^{\ast (C)$ is also an $SC^{\ast (X)$-module.}}
elements.sent-2411-168: The symplectic homology complex has the pairs of pants product and is hence an $SC^{\ast (X)$-module itself.}
elements.sent-2411-170: The open-closed map $\mathcal{OC}\colon \Ho CW^{\ast (C)\to SC^{\ast (X)$ is a map of $SC^{\ast (X)$-modules.}}}
elements.sent-2411-171: The boundary of the 1-dimensional (reduced) moduli spaces of curves with positive chord punctures at the boundary, one negative and one positive interior puncture on the distinguished ray from the center to the distinguished puncture correspond exactly to first multiplying and then applying the isomorphism or first applying the isomorphism and then multiplying.
elements.sent-2411-172: Together with a degeneration of moduli spaces argument this leads to the following result.
elements.sent-2411-173: The map $\mathcal{CO}\colon SC^{\ast (X)\to \Ho' CW^{\ast (C)$ is a quasi-isomorphism.}}
elements.sent-2411-174: We use two parallel fibers $C_0$ and $C_1$ as source of the map $\mathcal{OC}$ and target of the map $\mathcal{CO}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-175: We will take them to lie very close together.
elements.sent-2411-176: Note that for the Legendrians at infinity of the parallel fibers, the shift from $\partial_{\infty C_0$ to $\partial_{\infty C_{1}$ is induced by a Morse function on the sphere that is the restriction of a linear function that is negative at the south pole, vanishes on the equator, and positive at the north pole.}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Note then that Reeb chords $C_0\to C_1$ and $C_1\to C_0$ correspond to Reeb chords $C\to C$ and short Reeb chords near $C_0$, and that therefore, there is a natural 1-1 correspondence between Reeb chords $C_0\to C_{1}$ and Reeb chords $C_1\to C_0$.
elements.sent-2411-178: We remark that when we discuss moduli spaces of holomorphic curves for the Lagrangians $C_0$ and $C_{1}$ below, we need to use systems of parallel copies as described above.
elements.sent-2411-179: We use separate systems for $C_{0}$ and $C_{1}$, see \cite[Appendix B]{EL , but will leave these systems implicit in the notation.}
elements.sent-2411-181: In the complex $SC^\ast(X)$, $1$ is represented by the minimum of the Morse function on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-182: Since $\mathcal{CO}$ is an isomorphism we find $u\in \Ho CF^{\ast (C)$ such that $\mathcal{OC (u)=1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Take $s\in SC^{\ast (X)$ then since $\mathcal{OC}$ is a map of $SC^{\ast (X)$-modules with the module structure on $\Ho CF^{\ast (C)$ induced by $\mathcal{CO}$ followed by the natural action of $\Ho' CF^{\ast (C)$ on $\Ho CF^{\ast (C)$, we find \begin{equation}OC ( CO (s) \cdot u )= s\cdot OC(u)=s\cdot 1=s.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: To show that $\mathcal{CO}$ is surjective, note that $\mathcal{CO (1)$ counts curves with a positive puncture at the minimum on $X$.}
elements.sent-2411-186: The only such curves correspond to flow lines starting at the minimum and ending at a fixed point (the midpoint say) of any Reeb chords.
elements.sent-2411-187: It follows that \begin{equation}CO(1) \ = \ e \ =\sum_{\text{{ Reeb chords $c$}}} c_{pos}\otimes c_{neg}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-188: Consider the moduli spaces $\mathcal{M (u;e)$ involved in the equation \begin{equation}CO\circOC(u) = e.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-189: The elements in these moduli spaces are infinite length cylinders with positive punctures corresponding to $u$ at one boundary component and one positive and one negative puncture along the other boundary component corresponding to any chord in $e$.
elements.sent-2411-190: Gluing at the middle orbit we gain one dimension since the marker in the middle disappears.
elements.sent-2411-191: This means that the resulting moduli space has dimension two.
elements.sent-2411-192: Consider a cycle $r\in \Ho' CF^{\ast (C)$ and a non-trivial contribution to $r\cdot u$.}
elements.sent-2411-193: We use this contribution to degenerate the moduli spaces in $\mathcal{M (u;e)$.}
elements.sent-2411-194: We degenerate the annulus into two disks $D_{\mathrm{up }$ with positive punctures according to the positive punctures in $r$ and the positive puncture in the $e$-term corresponding to the negative puncture of $r$, and two negative punctures, and a disk $D_{\mathrm{dn }$ in the lower part with positive punctures at the chords in $r\cdot u$ that are not the negative chord of $r$.}}
elements.sent-2411-195: Under this deformation the moduli space undergoes additional splittings that will only affect the result up to exact terms, using the assumptions that $r$ and $u$ are cycles.
elements.sent-2411-196: We next glue $D_{\mathrm{up }$ and $D_{\mathrm{dn }$ via a cobordism corresponding to an isotopy that interchanges $\partial C_0$ and $\partial C_1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-197: The Lagrangian cobordism $T\subset \R\times\partial X$ corresponds to the Legendrian isotopy in $\partial X$ that rotates $\partial C_0$ to $\partial C_1$, i.e., and then takes the shifting function to its negative.
elements.sent-2411-198: It is easy to see that if $C_0$ and $C_1$ are sufficiently close and the rotation is sufficiently slow, all rigid holomorphic curves in the cobordism corresponds to (reparameterized) trivial Reeb chord strips of $c$ and strips (corresponding to Morse flow lines) that interchanges the small Reeb chords that follows the rotation.
elements.sent-2411-199: Let $\mathcal{M (T)$ denote the moduli space of rigid holomorphic curves with boundary on $T$.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Noting that the curves in $\mathcal{M (T)$ are strips that exactly give the natural 1-1 correspondence between Reeb chords $C_0\to C_1$ and $C_1\to C_0$, we find that the result of gluing $D_{\mathrm{dn }$ via $\mathcal{M (T)$ to $D_{\mathrm{up }$ are new annuli of dimension $2$ that have positive punctures according to $r\cdot u$ along one boundary component and positive and one negative puncture according to $r$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-201: Then deforming the Lagrangians to the standard $C_0\cup C_1$ and following the domains until it splits at a Reeb orbit, we find that \begin{equation}CO(OC(r\cdot u))=r,\end{equation} up to exact terms, see Figure \ref{fig:cardy .}
elements.sent-2411-202: It follows in particular that $\mathcal{CO}$ is surjective.. The theorem follows.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=.6\linewidth]{cardy} \caption{Degeneration of the composition $\mathcal{CO}\circ\mathcal{OC}(u)$ according to $r\in \Ho'CW^{\ast}(C)$ and gluing by the rotation cobordism.} \label{fig:cardy}
elements.subsection-2411-010: The symplectic homology product and Calabi-Yau structures
elements.sent-2411-203: In this section we consider the product on $SC^{\ast (X)$.}
elements.sent-2411-204: The isomorphism $\Phi^{SC}\colon SC^{\ast (X)\to \Ho CE^{\ast (\Lambda)$ allows us to push the pair of pants product $\mu$ on $SC^{\ast (X)$ to the Hochschild complex $\Ho CE^{\ast (\Lambda)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-205: This is related to a duality Calabi-Yau structure on $CE^{\ast (\Lambda)$.}
elements.sent-2411-206: In order to compute the product we consider the composition \begin{equation}\Phi^{SC}\circ \mu\colon SC^{\ast}(X)\otimes SC^{\ast}(X)\to \Ho CE^{\ast}(\Lambda).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-207: The curves contributing to this composition are disks with two positive punctures near the origin and negative boundary punctures and one extra puncture opposite the distinguished mixed puncture mapping to $z$.
elements.sent-2411-208: As in the proof of Theorem \ref{t: OC iso , at the gluing we gain one dimension from a disappearing conformal constraint.}
elements.sent-2411-209: We use it to control the conformal structure of the domain.
elements.sent-2411-210: Studying splittings we find the following, see \cite{BEE2 .}
elements.sent-2411-211: The symplectic cohomology product is represented on $\Ho CE^{\ast (\Lambda)$ by attaching disks with two positive punctures and auxiliary negative punctures to pairs of cyclic words of chords.}
elements.sent-2411-212: Here the disks are of two types: disks with one Lagrangian intersection puncture at $z$ and one positive and one negative puncture at the same Reeb chord $c$ for any $c$, and two level disks: a triangle followed by a disk with two positive punctures, see \cite[Figure 1]{BEE2 .\qed.}
elements.subsection-2411-011: Partial wrapping and coefficients in chains of the based loop space
elements.sent-2411-213: Starting from the study of attachments of punctured handles along knot conormals in \cite{ENS , a more systematic study of Legendrian surgery for partially wrapped Floer cohomology was undertaken in \cite{EL where it was conjectured that the partially wrapped Floer cohomology of a linking disk at a Legendrian stop is isomorphic to the Chekanov-Elishberg dg-algebra of the Legendrian stop with coefficients in chains on its based loop space.}}
elements.sent-2411-214: This result was later proved \cite[Theorem 1.2]{AE by translating everything to the surgery language.}
elements.sent-2411-215: This translation shows that any construction in exact Floer theory admits a surgery description and gives, for example, a rather direct and geometric proof of the cut and paste methods of \cite{GPS , see \cite[Theorem 1.3]{AE and \cite[Theorem 1.1]{A .}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: Here we will illustrate the idea by giving the definition of the surgery description of partially wrapped Floer cohomology and a sample result that can be proved with this technology.
elements.sent-2411-217: Consider Weinstein domains $W$ of dimension $2n$ and $V$ of dimension $2n-2$.
elements.sent-2411-218: We fix a Lagrangian skeleton $L$ with a fixed handle structure for $V$ and think of $V$ as a cotangent bundle of $L$.
elements.sent-2411-219: We take a Legendrian embedding of $L$ into $\partial W$ to be a contact embedding of a neighborhood $V\times (-\epsilon,\epsilon)$ of the zero section in the contactization of $V$.
elements.sent-2411-220: Given such an embedding, consider $W$ with the fixed embedding of $V\times (-\epsilon,\epsilon) $ in $\partial W$ and also the negative half $[0,-\infty)\times\R\times V$ of the symplectization of $\R\times V$ with $V\times (-\epsilon,\epsilon)\times V$ embedded in $\{0\}\times \R\times V$.
elements.sent-2411-221: We attach a $V$-handle, $T^{\ast I\times V$ to the union.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Note that the handles of $V$ give attaching spheres for $X$.
elements.sent-2411-223: We define the Chekanov-Eliahberg dg-algebra of $L$ to be the Chekanov-Eliashberg dg-algebra of the Legendrian attaching spheres of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-224: Note that it is generated by Reeb chords of $L$ together with Reeb chords of the Legendrian attaching spheres in $V$ in the middle of the handle.
elements.sent-2411-225: In case $L$ is smooth, observe that the internal Reeb chords of $L$ give a dg-algebra quasi-isomorphic to the chains on the based loop space of $L$.
elements.sent-2411-226: Using this observation one can show that the dg-algebra of $L$ then is isomorphic to the dg-algebra with loop space coefficients, see \cite[Theorem 1.2]{AE .}
elements.sent-2411-227: We give one illustration of how this construction can be used.
elements.sent-2411-228: Consider a smooth manifold $M$ and fix a Morse function $f\colon M\to\R$ with one maximum and one minimum.
elements.sent-2411-229: It is well known that the wrapped Floer cohomology of the cotangent fiber in $T^{\ast M$ is quasi-isomorphic to chains on the based loop space of $M$, \begin{equation}CW^{\ast}(T_{m}M)\approx C_{\ast}(\Omega M).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-230: For the purposes of this discussion, we think of this result as not cutting $M$ at all, or to comply with what comes next, as cutting $M$ below the minimum of the Morse function.
elements.sent-2411-231: We can also cut $M$ just below the maximum.
elements.sent-2411-232: If $M_{\le a}$ is the sublevel set then the cotangent bundle $T^{\ast M_{\le a}$, with corners rounded is a subcritical Weinstein domain with the level sphere $\Lambda_{a =\partial M_{\le a}$ as a Legendrian submanifold.}}
elements.sent-2411-233: By the surgery isomorphism, Theorem \ref{t:CW to CE , $CE^{\ast (\Lambda_{a )$ is also isomorphic to $CW^{\ast (T_{m M)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: It turns out the same holds true if we cut at any regular level $b$.
elements.sent-2411-235: More precisely, we get a subcritical Weinstein domain $T^{\ast M_{\le b}$ with a Legendrian level surface $\Lambda_{b =f^{-1 (b)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: Consider now the Chekanov-Eliashberg dg-algebra of $\Lambda_{b}$ with coefficients in chains of the based loop space of the super-level set $M_{\ge b}$, \begin{equation}CE^{\ast}(\Lambda_{b};C_{\ast}(\Omega(M_{\ge b}))).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-237: Then, in fact, \begin{equation}\label{eq:any reg} CE^{\ast}(\Lambda_{b};C_{\ast}(\Omega(M_{\ge b}))) \ \approx \ CW^{\ast}(T_{m}^{\ast}M)\end{equation} for every regular value $b$.
elements.sent-2411-238: The isomorphisms in \eqref{eq:any reg illustrate the fact that in the exact case, Floer cohomology can be expressed either in terms of topology or Reeb dynamics and further that it is possible to interpolate between these extreme cases by having a part in topology and another in Reeb dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-239: The isomorphisms in this example are straightforward to derive working in Legendrian surgery presentations.
elements.sent-2411-240: We describe them here as an illustration of the great generality and flexibility of the Eliashberg's original surgery approach to holomorphic curve theories in Weinstein domains.
elements.sent-2411-241: It can handle very general objects and provides e.g., Chekanov-Eliashberg dg-algebra or Floer cohomology for Legendrians with boundary with corners with coefficients in chains on the based loop space, in terms of the usual dg-algebras of Legendrian attaching spheres in suitable Weinstein manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-242: Such singular Lagrangians and Legendrians are very useful for instance as centers of mirror symmetry charts see \cite{D-RET for a simple example.}
